text: The context view essentially shows who the system talks to and what flows in and out.
The building block view is where we break the system down into its main components.
We've kept the runtime view focused on the happy path — error handling branches off separately.

glossary.meaning: the normal flow when everything works

text: On the deployment view, you can see what runs where and how it's all wired up.
We deliberately kept the diagrams at a fairly abstract level so they don't go out of date overnight.
If you want the full detail, that's all in the appendix — feel free to dig in afterwards.
What I'm showing you here is the SOLL state — the IST is in the backup slides.

glossary.meaning: extra slides for reference, not in the main flow

text: The boundaries of the system are drawn around what we actively control.
Anything sitting outside that boundary, we treat as a black box.

glossary.meaning: something whose internals we don't see/care about

text: I want to be upfront that some of the diagrams are still evolving as we get more feedback.
We used DDD selectively, mostly at the level where booking and payment interact.
Bounded contexts gave us a way to draw clear lines between different parts of the domain.
Within a bounded context, the team can speak its own language without stepping on anyone's toes.
Booking and payment are two separate aggregates, each with its own lifecycle.

glossary.meaning: a cluster of related domain objects treated as one unit (DDD)

text: We avoid sharing state directly between aggregates — communication goes through events.
The ubiquitous language is something we've tried to nail down with the operators themselves.
There's no point inventing fancy terminology if the business doesn't recognise it.
A booking goes through several states — pending, confirmed, paid, completed, refunded.
State-based management lets us reason clearly about what's allowed at each step.
If you're not careful with aggregates, you end up with a distributed monolith.
We picked PostgreSQL because it gave us the most flexibility without locking us into a vendor.

glossary.meaning: tie you to / make you dependent on

text: Going with a managed database lets us sleep at night.
Backups, patching, failover — all of that is handled for us, which frees up the team.
We're not at a scale where sharding makes sense yet.
Schema changes go through migrations — we don't touch production directly.

glossary.meaning: modify live systems without proper process

text: JSON columns have been a lifesaver for some of the more flexible fields.

glossary.meaning: extremely helpful / something that solves a problem

text: We're using soft deletes everywhere — nothing actually gets removed from the database.

glossary.meaning: marking a record as deleted without removing it

text: If we ever outgrow the current setup, we've left room to split things out later.
We've stuck with REST for now because everyone on the team is comfortable with it.
GraphQL was tempting, but the added complexity wasn't going to pay off at our scale.
Every endpoint is versioned, so we can evolve the API without breaking existing clients.
Authentication is handled by JWT tokens with a short expiry.
We try to keep the API surface as small as possible — fewer endpoints means fewer attack vectors.
OpenAPI specs are the source of truth — the code generates from them.
Rate limiting is in place to keep the system honest under load.

glossary.meaning: keep within its limits / prevent misuse

text: Idempotency keys make sure that retrying a payment request doesn't double-charge anyone.

glossary.meaning: property that repeating an operation gives the same result

text: We went with Next.js because server-side rendering matters for SEO on a travel site.
Vue was on the shortlist, but the team had more hands-on experience with React.
Angular felt like overkill for what we actually need.
We render the listing pages on the server so tourists don't see a blank screen first.

glossary.meaning: empty page (typical of slow client-side apps)

text: The booking flow is mostly client-side because it's highly interactive.
We don't ship any framework that we can't justify with a concrete need.
Performance budgets are something we actually enforce, not just talk about.
Mobile is where most of our traffic comes from, so we design for that first.
The booking process is the spine of the platform — everything else hangs off it.
On this BPMN diagram, you can see the happy path going straight across.
Anything that's not the happy path branches off — that keeps the diagram readable.
Payment confirmation is the trickiest part — there are several edge cases that need handling.

glossary.meaning: an unusual situation at the boundary of normal behaviour

text: Provider confirmation used to be unstructured, but the new flow makes it explicit.
We're moving from a manual back-and-forth to an instant-booking model.
If we can pull that off, the customer experience improves dramatically.
The payment flow has a built-in reimbursement path for when a booking falls through.
Cross-cutting concerns are the things that touch everything — logging, monitoring, security.
We've kept these consolidated rather than spreading them across each module.
Structured logging means we can actually search through what happened, not just scroll endlessly.

glossary.meaning: look through huge amounts of text without finding anything

text: Observability is something we under-invested in early on and have been catching up on since.

glossary.meaning: make up for lost time on / fix later

text: Tracing lets us follow a single request all the way through the system.
Without good metrics, you're flying blind when something starts to slow down.
Health checks are wired into the deployment pipeline — if something fails, the rollout stops.

glossary.meaning: the process of releasing a new version

text: We don't have a fancy SIEM, but we do log every authentication event.

glossary.meaning: Security Information and Event Management — security monitoring system

text: Everything ships through CI — there's no manual deployment anymore.
We do blue-green deployments to keep downtime to a minimum.
Rolling back is a one-click operation — that's saved us more than once.
Infrastructure is defined as code, so anyone on the team can spin up an environment.
We host on a managed cloud — the cost story works out cleaner that way.
Secrets live in a dedicated vault, never in the codebase.
Feature flags let us roll out changes gradually and pull them back if something breaks.
Staging mirrors production as closely as we can afford.
From a business standpoint, the key win here is faster booking turnaround.

glossary.meaning: looking at it from the business perspective

text: What this means in practice is operators spend less time chasing confirmations.
The technical side is one thing, but the impact on day-to-day operations is what really matters.
If we get this right, we should see the conversion rate move noticeably.
There's a clear ROI on cleaning up the booking flow, even before we get to the architecture benefits.
Stakeholder buy-in is half the work — the other half is building the thing.
We've been clear with the team about what's in scope and what's not.
I don't want to oversell this — there will be plenty of work after the architecture is in place.
The roadmap stays the same — what changes is how confidently we can deliver it.
At the end of the day, our job is to make sure the system can grow with the business.

glossary.word: at the end of the day
glossary.meaning: ultimately / when all is said and done

text: We've made a deliberate distinction between risks, non-risks, and sensitivity points.
A trade-off is a known compromise — a risk is an unknown that could bite us.
Documenting risks isn't about being negative — it's about being prepared.

glossary.meaning: be ready for what might come

text: For each risk, we've tried to capture a realistic mitigation plan.
Some risks we're choosing to accept rather than mitigate — that's a legitimate decision too.
The biggest risk we see is around third-party dependencies that we don't control.
Sendgrid and WhatsApp are essentially constraints we have to live with.
Budget itself is a constraint, not a risk — we know it, we plan around it.
If I had to summarise this in one sentence, it would be: making decisions explicit.
Bear in mind that what we're showing here is a snapshot — the system keeps evolving.

glossary.meaning: the state at a particular moment

text: I won't go through every single decision, but I'm happy to dig into any of them in Q&A.
Just before I move on, let me highlight one thing that often gets overlooked.
On a related note, this connects nicely to what I'll cover in the next section.
Stepping back for a second — what does all of this mean for the team in practice?

glossary.meaning: pause to look at the bigger picture

text: I want to flag something here that we haven't fully resolved yet.
Long story short — the system works, but we're documenting it properly for the first time.
We're fairly confident in this direction, though we'll keep validating it as we go.
I wouldn't claim this is the only valid approach — but it's the one we'd defend.

glossary.meaning: I'm not arguing / I'm not asserting

text: Reasonable people could disagree here, and that's fine.
My gut tells me this is the right call, but I want to back it up with data over time.
Take this with a slight grain of salt — the data set is still small.

glossary.word: take with a grain of salt

text: I'd rather flag uncertainty now than pretend everything's nailed down.

We deliberately limited ourselves to two because if everything's a priority, nothing is. Security and Performance Efficiency are the attributes that most directly shape architectural decisions for our current phase — the others stay on the radar, but they don't drive design.

glossary.word: if everything's a priority, nothing is
glossary.meaning: having too many top priorities is the same as having none

note: Q: Why did you elevate only two quality attributes to Priority 1?

text: Honestly, it came down to a mix of factors. Server-side rendering matters for SEO on a travel site, the team had more hands-on React experience, and the ecosystem fits where we're going. Angular felt like overkill, and Vue lost out on the team familiarity side.
note: Q: Why Next.js over Angular or Vue?

text: For a team of our size, microservices would have been overkill. We'd have spent more time on infrastructure than on features. We've kept the monolith but drawn clear internal boundaries, so we can split things out later if scale demands it.
note: Q: Why not break this up into microservices?

text: Yes, we use DDD selectively — particularly around booking and payment, which are the parts where the domain complexity is highest. We didn't apply it across the board because forcing DDD on simpler areas would have added more friction than value.
note: Q: Have you considered Domain-Driven Design seriously?

text: Fair point — availability is definitely on our radar, and I'd be the first to admit it's likely to bite us eventually. For now, the budget constraints mean we accept a degree of downtime risk, but we've documented this as a known sensitivity point.

glossary.word: I'd be the first to admit

note: Q: Availability seems pretty important for a tourism platform. Why isn't it Priority 1?

text: Payment is modelled as a state machine with an explicit refund flow built in. When a booking moves to cancelled, the payment object transitions through its reimbursement states and the customer gets refunded automatically, with the operator notified.

glossary.meaning: include as a core part of

note: Q: How do you handle payment reimbursement when a booking falls through?

text: Self-hosting would have meant carrying the operational burden of backups, patching, and failover ourselves. For a small team, the managed option lets us sleep at night and focus on features. It costs more in dollars, but less in stress.
note: Q: Why a managed database rather than self-hosting PostgreSQL?

text: We've kept costs deliberately low by sticking to managed services where the price-to-pain ratio is good, and self-managing only where we get real leverage. Cloud spend is a few hundred dollars a month at our current scale.

glossary.meaning: cost weighed against the trouble saved

note: Q: What's your cost story?

text: Right now, Perahuku is fairly loosely coupled — it feeds availability data into the booking platform asynchronously. We've made a deliberate choice to treat it as a system under study rather than baking it deeply into the main architecture.
note: Q: How does Perahuku integrate with the main booking platform?

text: Good question — we've flagged both as constraints rather than dependencies we control. If one goes down, the affected channel goes down with it. The architecture is structured so we could swap in an alternative provider relatively painlessly, but there'd still be a window of disruption.
note: Q: What happens if a third-party service like Sendgrid or WhatsApp disappears?

text: Honestly, peak season is something we're going to have to address head-on next year. Today we lean on the elasticity of the cloud provider, but the database is the bottleneck we'll hit first. We've sketched a caching strategy that should buy us a lot of headroom.
note: Q: How do you cope with peak season load?

text: Confidentiality is one of the two top quality attributes for exactly that reason. Personal data is encrypted at rest and in transit, access is role-based, and we never log sensitive fields. Payment data we deliberately keep out of our database entirely — it lives with the payment processor.

glossary.meaning: while stored and while being sent

note: Q: How do you protect tourist personal data?

text: I'll be honest — DR is still a work in progress. We have automated backups with point-in-time recovery, but we haven't done a full restore drill yet. That's on the roadmap for this quarter, and I'd put it at the top of the residual risk list.
note: Q: What's your disaster recovery plan?

text: We worked through them with the operators and a small group of regular customers. The scenarios come straight from real pain points they raised — we didn't invent them in a vacuum. That's also why we used the Bass et al. template, to keep them concrete and measurable.
note: Q: How did you validate the quality scenarios with stakeholders?

text: Probably keeping Perahuku as a separate system under study rather than merging it into the main platform. It would have been cleaner architecturally to bring them together, but Perahuku is still finding its audience, and locking it in too early would have been a mistake.
note: Q: What was the most controversial decision you made?

text: We did consider that early on, especially for the booking side. But the payment integration, the regulatory side, and the operator workflows all push beyond what no-code platforms handle gracefully. We'd have hit the limits within a few months.

glossary.meaning: reach the point where something stops working

note: Q: Couldn't this have just been a no-code or low-code solution?

text: We track the scenarios themselves — each one has a measurable target. Beyond that, we look at lead time for changes, incident rate, and how often the team has to argue about how things should be done. If those metrics trend the right way, the architecture is doing its job.
note: Q: How do you measure whether the architecture is succeeding?

text: Honestly, we don't have a formal one yet, and I'd rather be upfront about that than pretend otherwise. What we do have is documentation of where the debt lives, and a habit of paying small amounts down as we touch each area. The architecture work itself is a big down-payment.
note: Q: Do you have a tech debt strategy?

text: We're English-and-Indonesian for now, which covers our main markets. The architecture supports adding languages, but we haven't built it out beyond those two. Currency is handled separately — we always price in IDR and convert at checkout.
note: Q: How do you handle internationalisation?

text: Arc42 felt like the right balance of structure and flexibility for our context. C4 is excellent for visualising structure, but arc42 covers decisions, trade-offs, risks, and quality scenarios in one place. For a transferarbeit, that breadth mattered more than diagram polish.
note: Q: Why arc42 instead of C4 or another framework?

text: We use optimistic locking on the availability records. If two requests collide, the second one fails and the customer is gently told to pick another slot. It's rare in practice because the time windows are narrow, but the safety net is there.
note: Q: How do you handle a booking conflict — two customers booking the same slot?

text: We're a small team — two engineers and a designer, with operations support shared across the business. That team size has shaped pretty much every architectural call we've made.
note: Q: How big is your team?

text: The initial system was essentially a prototype that grew. We didn't start with formal prototyping, which is honestly part of why we're doing this architecture work now — to give a system that grew organically the deliberate shape it never had.
note: Q: Did you do any formal prototyping or was it all production from day one?

text: We rely on eventual consistency. Availability updates from Perahuku flow into the booking system asynchronously, and we accept a short lag window. For anything safety-critical — payments, refunds — we use synchronous calls with idempotency keys.
note: Q: How do you keep data consistent between Perahuku and the booking system?

text: Briefly. The cold-start latency was a concern for the search and booking paths, and the cost model gets unpredictable above a certain volume. We've kept it as a possible direction for specific workloads, but not as the default.
note: Q: Have you considered going serverless?

text: Search is one of our performance-critical paths. Right now it's database-backed with the right indexes, which gets us by at current scale. If volume grows, the plan is to introduce a search index — but we haven't pulled that trigger yet.
note: Q: How does the search work?

text: For European customers, yes — that was a non-negotiable from the start. We have a data processing record, right-to-erasure flows are in place, and personal data retention is bounded. The architecture had to support this from day one.
note: Q: Are you GDPR compliant?

text: The elephant in the room is probably that we have a single point of failure at the database layer. Multi-region replication is on the roadmap, but it's not implemented today, and a full outage at our cloud provider would take us down with it.

glossary.meaning: an obvious major issue everyone is avoiding
one component whose failure breaks everything

note: Q: What's the elephant in the room here?

text: Today, onboarding is largely a manual process — we sit down with each operator, walk them through Perahuku, and help them set up their first tours. It doesn't scale, but at our current operator count it lets us catch quality issues early.

glossary.meaning: won't work as the system grows

note: Q: How do operators get onboarded?

text: Realistically, we'd like to see Kapalsantai become the go-to platform for boat tourism in eastern Indonesia, with operators in two or three new regions and a much more automated booking flow. The architecture decisions we're making now are aimed at making that growth feasible.
note: Q: Where do you see Kapalsantai in two years?
